request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"knewton::edtech","name":"Knewton","one_liner":"Adaptive learning SaaS platform that personalized educational content for publishers, schools, and developers via a B2B API/licensing model.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both are B2B SaaS platforms that license their core technology to institutional customers (publishers/schools vs. academic spinouts/CROs). The licensing/subscription model is shared, but Knewton's customers were large educational publishers and universities, whereas the pitch targets smaller, more specialized biotech buyers."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"The markets are fundamentally different: Knewton operated in edtech/adaptive learning for higher education, while the pitch targets wet-lab assay management in biotech/life sciences. The only overlap is the 'B2B platform licensed to institutions' pattern."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both rely on a B2B licensing/partnership GTM — Knewton built its growth around large publisher partnerships (Pearson, Macmillan, HMH). The pitch similarly targets institutional buyers (academic spinouts, small CROs), though the channel and deal dynamics differ significantly given smaller target customers."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Knewton raised over $150M and reached Series F before being acquired for under $17M, reflecting over-capitalized hypergrowth ambitions. The pitch appears early-stage and niche-focused, but the SaaS platform licensing structure at founding stage has surface parallels."}},"why_similar":"Both are B2B SaaS platforms that license technology to institutional customers under a subscription/recurring revenue model. Both depend on the platform being adopted by organizations (not end-users directly), and both face the challenge of getting institutions to integrate a new workflow tool into existing processes. Knewton also positioned itself as infrastructure ('adaptive learning for any student') rather than a finished product — analogous to the pitch's assay-management platform being workflow infrastructure for labs.","where_diverged":"The pitch operates in an entirely different sector (biotech/life sciences wet-lab management vs. edtech adaptive learning). Target customers diverge sharply: academic spinouts and small CROs are smaller, more technically sophisticated buyers with tighter budgets than Knewton's large publishing house partners. The pitch does not appear to pursue large-enterprise publisher-scale partnerships. Additionally, the wet-lab assay domain has regulatory compliance requirements (e.g., GLP/GMP) that Knewton never faced, creating a moat possibility absent in edtech.","failure_causes":["over-reliance on a small number of large publisher partnerships","inability to convert platform usage into durable revenue at scale","acquired for under $17M after raising ~$157M — massive value destruction suggesting product-market fit was never firmly established","late pivot to direct courseware development diluted platform focus","heavy competition from incumbent LMS providers and publishers building in-house","customer dependency on third-party content owners limiting platform differentiation"],"lessons_for_input":["Avoid building your entire GTM around a handful of large institutional partners — diversify your customer base early to reduce concentration risk.","Validate that your platform genuinely replaces a painful workflow before scaling; Knewton's adaptive engine never clearly proved indispensable to publishers.","Stay focused on the platform or the product — not both; Knewton's late pivot to courseware confused its positioning and diluted engineering resources.","Target customers who own the problem budget (small CROs control their own software spend, unlike university IT departments), which can reduce the sales cycle length that killed Knewton.","Price and contract to capture value commensurate with the capital raised; Knewton's exit under $17M on $157M raised suggests its monetization never matched its cost structure."]}
